stem: A 4-year-old child is brought to a regional emergency department in Tamworth (Hunter New England LHD) after ingesting an unknown quantity of an over-the-counter medication that acts as a competitive, lipophilic antagonist at central and peripheral muscarinic acetylcholine receptors. There is no nicotinic activity. The agent crosses the blood-brain barrier readily. Onset was approximately 60 minutes ago. The grandparent who called the ambulance is unsure what was taken; the empty blister pack has been brought in but the label is unreadable.

data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable, mumbling speech, temperature 38.4 degrees Celsius, HR 148, BP 102/64, dry axillae, flushed dry skin, dilated minimally reactive pupils, bowel sounds absent, bladder palpable.
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.36, lactate 2.1 mmol/L, glucose 6.2 mmol/L, paracetamol level <10 micromol/L, salicylate not detected, ECG sinus tachycardia QRS 88 ms QTc 440 ms.

lead_in: Based purely on the receptor mechanism described, which clinical feature is the single best discriminator from a sympathomimetic toxidrome?

options.text: Dry flushed skin with absent bowel sounds and urinary retention
options.rationale: Muscarinic antagonism blocks parasympathetic outflow to exocrine glands (anhidrosis with cutaneous vasodilation for heat loss producing flushing), gut smooth muscle (ileus), and detrusor (retention). Sympathomimetic toxidromes produce diaphoresis and active bowel sounds because cholinergic sweat innervation and gut activity are preserved.

options.text: Tachycardia with hypertension and mydriasis
options.rationale: Tachycardia and mydriasis occur in both toxidromes; hypertension is more sympathomimetic but is not discriminatory in early anticholinergic toxicity. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: Hyperthermia with agitated delirium
options.rationale: Both toxidromes can produce hyperthermia and agitation; this is non-discriminating. Would be wrong (Source: Murray's Toxicology Handbook).

options.text: Pinpoint pupils with bradycardia and bronchorrhoea
options.rationale: This is the cholinergic (muscarinic agonist) toxidrome, the opposite of what is described. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: Hypotension with miosis and hyporeflexia
options.rationale: Describes opioid or sedative-hypnotic toxidrome, not a muscarinic mechanism. Would be wrong (Source: NSW PIC).

explanation.summary: Pure muscarinic antagonism produces a syndrome that copies sympathomimetic toxicity in vital signs and pupils but separates on exocrine and smooth muscle function. Sweat glands are anatomically sympathetic but pharmacologically cholinergic (muscarinic) so antimuscarinic toxicity produces anhidrosis with reflex flushing rather than diaphoresis. Gut and bladder are tonically parasympathetic so blockade produces ileus and retention rather than hyperactive bowel sounds. These four features are the bedside discriminator.
key_points: Anticholinergic = hot, dry, flushed, blind, mad, full bladder, gut stops
Sympathomimetic = hot, WET, agitated, normal bowel sounds
Sweat innervation is sympathetic anatomy but muscarinic pharmacology
QRS widening suggests sodium-channel blockade overlap (TCA, diphenhydramine)
Call NSW PIC 13 11 26 for paediatric ingestion advice
explanation.pearls: Common culprits in children: diphenhydramine, atropine eye drops, hyoscine patches, Datura/Brugmansia ingestion in older kids. Physostigmine (centrally acting AChE inhibitor) is reserved for severe agitated delirium; not first line in Australia.

subtopic_detail: Anticholinergic vs sympathomimetic toxidrome discrimination by mechanism

stem: A 24-year-old woman presents to her GP with progressive cosmetic concerns over the past 9 months. She has noticed coarse facial hair growth requiring weekly waxing, gum swelling that bleeds when she brushes, and her partner has noticed she sounds slightly slurred in the evenings. She also reports new acne, intermittent diplopia at night, and unsteadiness walking down stairs. She started a new daily medication 11 months ago for a chronic condition diagnosed in adolescence. She is not on any other regular medication and is not pregnant.

data_table.Examination: Hirsutism over chin and sideburns, gingival hyperplasia covering proximal third of lower incisors with contact bleeding, mild horizontal nystagmus on lateral gaze, broad-based gait, finger-nose with end-point intention tremor.
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, LFTs ALT 65 U/L (slightly raised), GGT 110 U/L, urinalysis negative, beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which medication is the single most likely cause of this constellation?

options.text: Phenytoin
options.rationale: The classic quartet of gingival hyperplasia, hirsutism, cerebellar signs (nystagmus, ataxia, intention tremor), and raised GGT is pathognomonic for chronic phenytoin therapy, mediated by altered fibroblast collagen turnover, androgenic effects, dose-dependent cerebellar toxicity, and hepatic enzyme induction. Coarsening of facial features is also recognised.

options.text: Ciclosporin
options.rationale: Causes gingival hyperplasia and hirsutism but not cerebellar signs or nystagmus; would also expect hypertension and renal impairment. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Amlodipine
options.rationale: Calcium-channel blockers cause gingival hyperplasia but do not cause hirsutism or cerebellar ataxia. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Sodium valproate
options.rationale: Causes tremor, weight gain, hair LOSS (alopecia) not hirsutism, and hepatotoxicity; does not cause gingival hyperplasia. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Topiramate
options.rationale: Causes cognitive slowing, paraesthesiae, weight loss and renal stones; no gingival hyperplasia or hirsutism. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

explanation.summary: Three drug classes cause gingival hyperplasia in Australian practice: phenytoin, ciclosporin, and dihydropyridine calcium-channel blockers (amlodipine, nifedipine). Phenytoin is the only one that ALSO causes the cerebellar triad and hirsutism. The discriminator is the combination, not any single feature. Modern practice has shifted to levetiracetam, lamotrigine and valproate for generalised epilepsy, but phenytoin remains in use for status epilepticus and in patients stable on long-standing therapy.
key_points: Gingival hyperplasia: phenytoin > ciclosporin > CCBs
Hirsutism + gingival hyperplasia = phenytoin or ciclosporin
Add cerebellar signs = phenytoin
GGT rise reflects enzyme induction, not hepatotoxicity
Consider switching to levetiracetam if cosmetic burden is significant
explanation.pearls: Phenytoin follows zero-order kinetics in the therapeutic range; a small dose increase can tip into toxicity. Free phenytoin level is more useful than total in hypoalbuminaemia or pregnancy.

subtopic_detail: Adverse effect cluster mapping to anticonvulsant drug class

stem:
A 33-year-old woman is day 5 postpartum after a spontaneous vaginal birth at 39 weeks, complicated by gestational hypertension treated antenatally with labetalol. She is exclusively breastfeeding her well term infant. She represents to her GP with persistent BP readings of 152/98 to 158/102 on three separate occasions. She has a documented angioedema reaction to enalapril 4 years ago. She has stage 3a chronic kidney disease (eGFR 48) from reflux nephropathy diagnosed in childhood, well controlled gout for which she takes allopurinol, and she has asthma requiring a salbutamol inhaler twice weekly with one ICU admission 3 years ago for a severe exacerbation.

Her current labetalol 200 mg orally bd is not controlling her BP and she reports worsening fatigue and bronchospasm with exertion that she attributes to labetalol.

data_table.Examination: BP 156/100, HR 78, no oedema, chest clear, no headache, no visual disturbance, no epigastric pain, no hyperreflexia.
data_table.Investigations: Creatinine 130 micromol/L (baseline 115), eGFR 48, urine ACR 8 mg/mmol, FBC normal, LFTs normal, urate 0.42 mmol/L, urine protein:creatinine ratio 25 mg/mmol.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate antihypertensive substitution?

options.text: Nifedipine modified-release 30 mg orally once daily
options.rationale: Dihydropyridine calcium-channel blockers are recommended in the postpartum period by SOMANZ and RANZCOG, are compatible with breastfeeding (negligible milk transfer), do not exacerbate asthma, do not require renal dose adjustment at eGFR 48, and avoid the cross-reactive angioedema risk of ACEi/ARBs.

options.text: Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg orally once daily
options.rationale: Thiazides reduce milk supply in the first weeks postpartum, raise urate (problematic with her gout), and have reduced efficacy at eGFR <45. Would be wrong (Source: SOMANZ).

options.text: Perindopril 4 mg orally once daily
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors are otherwise breastfeeding-compatible but are contraindicated in this woman due to documented enalapril-induced angioedema; cross-reactivity between ACEi members is high. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Atenolol 50 mg orally once daily
options.rationale: Cardioselective beta-blocker but still relatively contraindicated in severe asthma with prior ICU admission; atenolol also concentrates in breast milk more than labetalol or metoprolol. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Methyldopa 250 mg orally tds
options.rationale: Methyldopa is used antenatally but is not recommended postpartum due to its sedative effect and association with postnatal depression. Would be wrong (Source: SOMANZ).

explanation.summary: Each constraint independently excludes one option. ACEi angioedema history rules out perindopril. Severe asthma rules out atenolol. CKD-3a and gout rule out thiazide. Postpartum status rules out methyldopa. Only nifedipine MR satisfies all constraints. The pattern-matching skill is to enumerate constraints and cross off rather than match a single feature.
key_points: Postpartum HTN first-line: nifedipine MR or labetalol
ACEi angioedema cross-reacts within class (enalapril -> perindopril)
Thiazides reduce milk supply early postpartum
Methyldopa associated with postnatal depression - avoid
Severe asthma with prior ICU is relative contraindication to beta-blockade
explanation.pearls: Postpartum BP often peaks at days 3-6 due to fluid mobilisation; review at days 3-5 and 7-10 is standard. Persistent HTN beyond 12 weeks needs investigation for secondary causes.

subtopic_detail: Antihypertensive choice with stacked breastfeeding, CKD, asthma, ACEi-allergy, gout constraints

stem:
A 22-year-old man is brought to ED at John Hunter Hospital after using crystal methamphetamine at a nightclub 90 minutes ago. He is agitated and combative. Initial parenteral midazolam 5 mg IV has reduced agitation partially. His BP remains markedly elevated and he is now developing chest pain radiating to the left arm with diaphoresis. ECG shows ST elevation in the inferolateral leads.

Bedside echo shows preserved LV function and no regional wall motion abnormality at rest. The cardiology registrar asks for pharmacological management of his hypertension prior to angiography, recognising the agent he has used is a presynaptic monoamine releaser causing massive endogenous noradrenaline release acting at both alpha-1 and beta-1 receptors.

data_table.Examination: Agitated but redirectable after midazolam, BP 198/118, HR 142 sinus, temperature 38.1, diaphoretic, mydriatic pupils 6 mm, chest pain 7/10 retrosternal.
data_table.Investigations: Troponin I 180 ng/L (rising), ECG ST elevation II, III, aVF and V5-V6 with reciprocal change, urine drug screen positive amphetamines, ABG pH 7.32 lactate 4.1 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which agent is the single most appropriate first-line for blood pressure control in this setting?

options.text: Glyceryl trinitrate infusion titrated to BP and chest pain
options.rationale: In sympathomimetic-induced coronary vasospasm, nitrates relieve epicardial spasm directly and lower BP via venodilation without unopposed alpha effects. Australian toxicology guidance (NSW PIC, eTG) endorses benzodiazepines first, then GTN and phentolamine for ongoing hypertension or chest pain in cocaine/amphetamine ACS. Beta-blockers are avoided because of the theoretical risk of unopposed alpha-mediated coronary vasoconstriction.
source_refs: NSW Poisons Information Centre Amphetamine toxicity

options.text: Metoprolol 5 mg IV
options.rationale: Non-selective and selective beta-blockade is relatively contraindicated in sympathomimetic ACS because of the unopposed-alpha hypothesis; in Australian practice labetalol (mixed alpha-beta) is preferred over pure beta-blockade if a beta-blocker is essential. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: Atenolol 25 mg orally
options.rationale: Same concern as metoprolol; oral route also inappropriate in acute coronary syndrome with active hypertension. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Adrenaline 1 mg IV
options.rationale: Adrenaline would worsen the sympathomimetic state catastrophically; not indicated. Would be wrong (Source: ARC Guidelines).

options.text: Verapamil 5 mg IV
options.rationale: Non-dihydropyridine CCB has negative inotropic effect risky in ACS with possible LV strain; not first-line. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

explanation.summary: The mechanism-counter-mechanism logic: methamphetamine causes massive monoamine release with both alpha-1 (vasoconstriction, coronary spasm) and beta-1 (chronotropy, contractility) stimulation. The dangerous component in ACS is alpha-1 coronary vasoconstriction. Pure beta-blockade leaves alpha-1 unopposed which can worsen spasm; benzodiazepines lower central sympathetic outflow indirectly, GTN relieves spasm directly, and phentolamine provides pure alpha-1 blockade if needed. The discriminator is recognising why beta-blockade alone is contraindicated.
key_points: Sympathomimetic ACS first-line: benzodiazepines + GTN
Avoid pure beta-blockade; labetalol acceptable if beta-blocker essential
Phentolamine for refractory alpha-mediated HTN
Cooling for hyperthermia >39 degrees Celsius
Consult NSW PIC 13 11 26 early
explanation.pearls: The unopposed-alpha concern is best supported in cocaine ACS but extended to amphetamine by Australian guidelines. If the patient needs a beta-blocker for tachyarrhythmia, labetalol is the agent of choice because it has alpha-1 blockade built in.

subtopic_detail: Pharmacological reversal logic in sympathomimetic ACS

stem: A 28-year-old woman, G2P0 with one previous first-trimester miscarriage, presents to the Early Pregnancy Assessment Service at the Royal Hospital for Women in Sydney with 4 days of right iliac fossa pain and minimal PV spotting. LMP was 7 weeks and 2 days ago. She is haemodynamically stable, has no shoulder tip pain, and her abdomen is soft with right iliac fossa tenderness only. She has completed her family and would prefer non-surgical management if appropriate.

data_table.Examination: BP 116/72, HR 84, abdomen soft, RIF tender, no peritonism, cervix closed on speculum, minimal old blood in vault.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 4800 IU/L (rose from 2900 IU/L over 48 hours), FBC Hb 128 g/L, LFTs normal, creatinine 62 micromol/L, blood group A positive. Transvaginal ultrasound: empty uterus, right adnexal mass 28 mm with surrounding free fluid in pouch of Douglas measuring 12 mL, no foetal pole within the adnexal mass.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management decision based on Australian criteria?

options.text: Laparoscopic salpingectomy after consent
options.rationale: RANZCOG and ASUM criteria for methotrexate eligibility require beta-hCG <5000 IU/L AND adnexal mass <35 mm AND no foetal cardiac activity AND no significant free fluid AND patient able to comply with follow-up. Her beta-hCG of 4800 is below 5000 but the rising trend (>50% over 48 hours indicating active trophoblast) combined with 12 mL free fluid in Douglas reduces methotrexate success and increases rupture risk. Surgery is the safer pathway, particularly as she has completed her family.

options.text: Single-dose methotrexate 50 mg per square metre IM
options.rationale: Free fluid in Douglas plus a rapidly rising beta-hCG reduce methotrexate efficacy below acceptable thresholds despite the beta-hCG number itself being below 5000. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCOG).

options.text: Expectant management with serial beta-hCG every 48 hours
options.rationale: Expectant management requires falling beta-hCG and an asymptomatic patient; her hCG is rising and she is symptomatic. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCOG).

options.text: Laparoscopic salpingotomy with tube preservation
options.rationale: Salpingotomy is offered when contralateral tube is damaged and future fertility is desired; she has a normal contralateral tube and has completed her family. Salpingectomy is preferred to reduce persistent trophoblast risk. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat ultrasound in 7 days
options.rationale: Delays definitive management in a symptomatic patient with confirmed ectopic features; rupture risk is unacceptable. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Australian methotrexate eligibility requires ALL of: beta-hCG <5000 IU/L, adnexal mass <35 mm, no foetal cardiac activity, no significant free fluid (>50 mL by some criteria; any free fluid lowers success), reliable follow-up, normal renal/hepatic function, no breastfeeding, no immune compromise. Even when the number is below 5000, a rapidly rising trend and any free fluid shift the risk calculus toward surgery. The discriminator is recognising that thresholds are a checklist, not a single number.
key_points: MTX threshold: beta-hCG <5000 IU/L AND <35 mm mass AND no FH AND no significant fluid
Rising hCG predicts MTX failure even below the 5000 cutoff
Salpingectomy preferred over salpingotomy when contralateral tube normal
Anti-D 250 IU IM if Rh negative regardless of management
Day 4 and day 7 hCG required if MTX given
explanation.pearls: ASUM consensus: discriminatory zone for intrauterine pregnancy on TVUS is beta-hCG 1500-2000 IU/L. Empty uterus above this level with no adnexal pathology raises concern for ectopic or a complete miscarriage.

subtopic_detail: Methotrexate threshold criteria for ectopic pregnancy management

stem:
A 58-year-old retiree from the Central Coast presents to a Gosford respiratory clinic with 6 months of progressive dyspnoea, dry cough and 4 kg weight loss. He is a lifelong non-smoker. He has been retired for 5 years from his job as an aircraft mechanic. He spends most of his time at home; he keeps a small allotment vegetable garden, has two outdoor cats, and tends to the racing pigeon loft his neighbour asked him to mind during a 12-month overseas trip that began 8 months ago.

He has no occupational asbestos exposure beyond brief brake-pad work in the 1980s. There is no fever, no haemoptysis, no joint pain, no rash. Resting SpO2 96 percent room air, falling to 88 percent after a 6-minute walk test. Fine bibasal end-inspiratory crackles are heard but no clubbing. Spirometry shows a restrictive pattern with FEV1/FVC 0.84, TLC 71 percent predicted, DLCO 52 percent predicted. CT chest reports patchy ground-glass and centrilobular nodularity with mid-to-upper lobe predominance.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C, HR 92, RR 22, BP 132/82, SpO2 96% room air resting (88% post 6MWT).
data_table.Examination: Fine bibasal crackles, no clubbing, no rash, no synovitis.
data_table.Investigations: Spirometry restrictive (FEV1/FVC 0.84, TLC 71%, DLCO 52%), CT chest patchy ground-glass + centrilobular nodularity, mid-upper lobe predominant. FBC, UEC, LFT, CRP all normal. ANA, ENA, ANCA negative.

lead_in: Which of the following is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF)
options.rationale: IPF is basal-predominant with honeycombing and reticulation, age >60 with insidious onset, and would show a UIP pattern on CT - not mid-upper-lobe ground glass with centrilobular nodules. Source: TSANZ/Lung Foundation Australia IPF guidelines.

options.text: Chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis from avian antigen exposure
options.rationale: The discriminator is the pigeon loft, which the candidate must pick out from a long social history. Avian antigens (bird droppings, feathers, bloom proteins) drive a type III/IV hypersensitivity reaction producing centrilobular nodularity and ground-glass with mid-upper-lobe predominance - exactly the imaging described. Restrictive PFTs with reduced DLCO, fine crackles without clubbing in a non-smoker fit. Source: TSANZ Interstitial Lung Disease Position Statement; Cormier 2016.

options.text: Asbestos-related pleural disease
options.rationale: Brief brake-pad exposure 40 years ago is below threshold for asbestos disease, and asbestos causes pleural plaques or asbestosis with basal reticulation - not centrilobular nodularity. Source: ATS/ERS Asbestos guidelines.

options.text: Sarcoidosis stage II
options.rationale: Sarcoidosis would typically show bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy with upper-lobe interstitial change and is age 20-40 in most Australian cohorts; ACE level and Ca often abnormal. The imaging here lacks lymphadenopathy and ANA/ENA are non-discriminating. Source: TSANZ Sarcoidosis Guidelines.

options.text: Cryptogenic organising pneumonia (COP)
options.rationale: COP usually has patchy peripheral or peribronchovascular consolidation rather than centrilobular nodularity, and responds rapidly to steroids - the discriminator is the imaging pattern + occupational/animal history. Source: eTG Respiratory.

explanation.summary: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP) is driven by inhaled organic antigens (avian proteins, mould, isocyanates) producing a non-IgE immune reaction in alveoli and bronchioles. The chronic form looks like restrictive ILD; the discriminator is exposure history. Mid-upper-lobe predominance and centrilobular nodules differ from IPF (basal, reticular, honeycomb) and sarcoid (lymphadenopathy). Antigen avoidance is the central management step - even before steroids - because continued exposure causes irreversible fibrosis.
key_points: Bird-fancier's lung is the prototypical chronic HP exposure
Mid-upper-lobe centrilobular nodules + ground glass = HP pattern
Restrictive PFTs with low DLCO and exercise desaturation
Antigen avoidance is mandatory and primary therapy
Precipitating IgG (against pigeon serum) supports but does not exclude
explanation.pearls: Always ask explicitly about birds, hot tubs, mould exposure, and farming - patients rarely volunteer these on standard history. The Central Coast and Hunter regions have an active pigeon-racing culture, making this a regionally relevant differential.

sources.label: TSANZ Interstitial Lung Disease Position Statement

subtopic_detail: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis from avian antigen exposure

stem: A 12-month-old infant weighing 9.4 kg is brought to John Hunter Hospital paediatric ED 90 minutes after the parent inadvertently gave the child five doses of paracetamol 250 mg/5 mL suspension instead of paracetamol 120 mg/5 mL suspension, at the prescribed paediatric volume of 6 mL per dose, over the past 16 hours (one dose every 4 hours; the 5th dose was 90 minutes ago). The child is alert, well, afebrile, drinking, and has no abdominal pain or vomiting. No other agent was co-ingested. Pre-existing health is unremarkable. The parent has brought the bottle and the dosing syringe.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 124, RR 28, BP 92/58, T 36.9 deg C, SpO2 99% room air.
data_table.Examination: Alert, hydrated, abdomen soft, no hepatomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: Pending - clinician calculating total ingested dose to determine NAC eligibility.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management based on the calculated cumulative dose?

options.text: Discharge with safety-netting; cumulative dose below the staggered-ingestion treatment threshold
options.rationale: The calculation: 5 doses x 6 mL x 250 mg/5 mL = 5 x 300 mg = 1500 mg total over 16 hours = 1500 / 9.4 = 159.6 mg/kg. The Australian Poisons Information Centre threshold for repeated supratherapeutic ingestion in children is >150 mg/kg in any 24-hour period (or >100 mg/kg/day for >2 days). This child is above threshold; discharge is wrong. Source: Murray's Toxicology Handbook; NSW PIC.

options.text: Commence IV N-acetylcysteine using the 21-hour Prescott regimen and check paracetamol concentration and ALT now
options.rationale: Cumulative dose is 159.6 mg/kg in <24 hours (5 x 6 mL x 50 mg/mL = 1500 mg; 1500/9.4 = 159.6 mg/kg). This exceeds the 150 mg/kg/24h repeated supratherapeutic threshold. The nomogram does not apply to staggered ingestion; treatment decision is based on dose plus ALT/paracetamol level. NAC is started empirically while bloods are awaited. The 21-hour Prescott regimen (150-50-100 mg/kg) is current Australian standard. Source: NSW Poisons Information Centre; Murray's Toxicology Handbook; eTG Toxicology.

options.text: Use the Rumack-Matthew nomogram at 4 hours post the most recent dose to decide NAC
options.rationale: The nomogram is invalid in staggered or repeated supratherapeutic ingestion - it was derived for single acute ingestions only. Source: NSW PIC.

options.text: Administer activated charcoal 1 g/kg orally to limit further absorption
options.rationale: Charcoal is useful only within 1-2 hours of an acute single ingestion, not for staggered ingestion of a now-mostly-absorbed dose. Source: eTG Toxicology.

options.text: Repeat paracetamol level at 24 hours post the most recent dose and decide on NAC then
options.rationale: Delaying NAC past 8 hours from ingestion significantly increases hepatotoxicity risk. Empirical NAC should start now and be discontinued only if labs clearly fall below treatment threshold. Source: NSW PIC.

explanation.summary: The discriminator is the unit-conversion trap: the family used a 250 mg/5 mL strength instead of 120 mg/5 mL, giving each 6 mL dose 300 mg of paracetamol rather than the intended 144 mg. Five doses over 16 hours = 1500 mg = 159.6 mg/kg in a 9.4 kg child. Australian staggered-ingestion threshold is 150 mg/kg/24h - this child is above. The Rumack-Matthew nomogram applies only to single acute ingestions; for staggered ingestion, decision is based on cumulative dose, ALT, and paracetamol level interpreted in clinical context.
key_points: Staggered paracetamol ingestion: nomogram does NOT apply
AU threshold for treatment in children: >150 mg/kg/24h or >100 mg/kg/day for >2 days
Paracetamol suspension strengths in AU: 120 mg/5 mL (infant) and 250 mg/5 mL (older child) - common dispensing error
Empirical NAC then de-escalate based on bloods is the safe pathway
Australian Prescott 21-hour regimen: 150 mg/kg over 1 h, then 50 mg/kg over 4 h, then 100 mg/kg over 16 h
explanation.pearls: Always confirm the strength of liquid paediatric paracetamol with the parent and look at the bottle. Two-strength availability in Australia is a recurring source of accidental supratherapeutic ingestion.

subtopic_detail: Paracetamol staggered supratherapeutic ingestion threshold

stem:
A 22-year-old woman presents to a Newcastle hepatology clinic with 6 weeks of fatigue, mild jaundice and a 3 kg weight loss. She is not on any medication and denies herbal supplement use. Alcohol intake is 2 standard drinks per week. She has no risk factors for viral hepatitis on screening interview. There is a remote history of vitiligo and a paternal history of type 1 diabetes mellitus.

Bloods today show ALT 612 U/L, AST 540 U/L, ALP 142 U/L, GGT 88 U/L, total bilirubin 58 micromol/L, conjugated 41 micromol/L, INR 1.2, albumin 36 g/L, IgG 26 g/L (high). Viral hepatitis serology (HBsAg, anti-HCV, anti-HAV IgM, anti-HEV IgM) negative; HIV negative. Caeruloplasmin and alpha-1-antitrypsin normal. Iron studies show normal ferritin and transferrin saturation. Autoimmune panel: ANA 1:640 (homogeneous), anti-smooth muscle antibody (ASMA) 1:160, anti-LKM-1 negative, anti-mitochondrial antibody (AMA) negative, anti-soluble liver antigen (SLA) negative.

data_table.Examination: Mild scleral icterus, no stigmata of chronic liver disease, soft non-tender abdomen, no hepatosplenomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: Hepatocellular pattern (ALT 612, AST 540, ALP 142). Bilirubin 58 (conj 41). IgG 26 (high). ANA 1:640 homogeneous, ASMA 1:160, anti-LKM-1 negative, AMA negative.

lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Primary biliary cholangitis
options.rationale: PBC is cholestatic (ALP-dominant, raised GGT, elevated IgM) with positive AMA in 95 percent. Here ALT/AST dominate, GGT is only mildly elevated, IgG (not IgM) is high, and AMA is negative. Source: eTG Gastrointestinal.

options.text: Type 1 autoimmune hepatitis
options.rationale: The pattern fits AIH type 1: young woman, hepatocellular injury (ALT > ALP), markedly elevated IgG, ANA homogeneous + ASMA positive (the type 1 serology), AMA and anti-LKM-1 negative. The associated autoimmunity (vitiligo, family T1DM) supports. International AIH Group simplified criteria score >=7 (definite). Source: eTG Gastrointestinal; AASLD AIH Practice Guidance.

options.text: Type 2 autoimmune hepatitis
options.rationale: AIH type 2 has anti-LKM-1 or anti-LC1 antibodies (negative here) and typically presents in childhood/adolescence. ASMA-positive AIH is type 1. Source: AASLD.

options.text: Primary sclerosing cholangitis
options.rationale: PSC is cholestatic with bile-duct strictures on imaging; ALP > ALT; pANCA positive in 65 percent. Pattern here is hepatocellular and ANCA was not even part of the differential. Source: eTG Gastrointestinal.

options.text: Wilson disease
options.rationale: Wilson is excluded by normal caeruloplasmin and presents with mixed neurological/psychiatric features and Kayser-Fleischer rings. AIH-like ANA + ASMA in Wilson is rare. Source: eTG Gastrointestinal.

explanation.summary: AIH is a T-cell-mediated attack on hepatocytes producing a hepatocellular injury pattern (ALT/AST elevation), polyclonal hypergammaglobulinaemia (IgG specifically), and characteristic autoantibodies. Type 1 (ANA + ASMA positive, anti-LKM-1 negative) accounts for ~75 percent of cases and presents in young adults, often with associated autoimmunity (thyroid, vitiligo, T1DM). The IAIHG simplified score combines autoantibody titre, IgG level, biopsy histology and exclusion of viral hepatitis. Treatment is prednisolone +/- azathioprine; biopsy is recommended before commencing to grade activity and exclude overlap syndromes.
key_points: Type 1 AIH = ANA + ASMA (anti-LKM-1 negative); type 2 = anti-LKM-1 or anti-LC1
Hepatocellular pattern + raised IgG + ANA/ASMA in a young woman = AIH until proven otherwise
Personal or family history of other autoimmune disease supports
Biopsy: interface hepatitis + plasma cell infiltrate confirms
Treatment: prednisolone 30-60 mg, taper, add azathioprine; long-term remission >80%
explanation.pearls: Beware overlap syndromes (AIH-PBC, AIH-PSC) - if ALP is disproportionately high or AMA is positive, image the biliary tree and consider MRCP.

subtopic_detail: Autoimmune hepatitis type 1 serological pattern

stem:
A 67-year-old man presents to a Maitland general practice with 5 days of progressively severe bilateral thigh and shoulder muscle pain, dark cola-coloured urine and weakness when climbing stairs. He cannot recall recent strenuous exercise or trauma. He has hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypothyroidism and stable HFrEF. His regular medications are atorvastatin 40 mg, perindopril 5 mg, metformin XR 1 g BD, levothyroxine 100 micrograms, and bisoprolol 5 mg, all unchanged for over 2 years.

Two weeks ago his GP started clarithromycin 500 mg BD for a 7-day course to treat a confirmed Helicobacter pylori eradication regimen. The macrolide course finished 5 days ago. Bloods today: CK 38400 U/L, creatinine 142 micromol/L (baseline 80), K 4.1, urine dipstick blood ++++, microscopy 2 RBC per high power field, TSH 2.4.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.5, HR 78, BP 132/78, SpO2 98% room air.
data_table.Examination: Bilateral proximal muscle tenderness in shoulders and thighs, power 4/5 against resistance, reflexes preserved, no rash.
data_table.Investigations: CK 38 400 U/L; Cr 142 (baseline 80); K 4.1; urine dipstick blood ++++ but only 2 RBC/HPF on microscopy (myoglobinuria); TSH 2.4.

lead_in: Which is the single most likely precipitant for this presentation?

options.text: Hypothyroid-induced myopathy from drift in levothyroxine effect
options.rationale: TSH is normal at 2.4, excluding overt hypothyroid myopathy. Source: eTG Endocrinology.

options.text: Clarithromycin-induced atorvastatin toxicity via CYP3A4 inhibition
options.rationale: Clarithromycin is a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor and atorvastatin is a CYP3A4 substrate; co-administration causes a several-fold rise in plasma statin and risk of rhabdomyolysis. The TIME-OF-ONSET is the discriminator: symptoms started during/after the macrolide course on an otherwise unchanged statin regimen of >2 years. Eradication therapy is a recognised trigger. Australian Medicines Handbook and eTG both list this interaction. Source: AMH 2024; eTG Cardiovascular.

options.text: Idiopathic immune-mediated necrotising myopathy (anti-HMGCR)
options.rationale: Anti-HMGCR necrotising myopathy is a recognised but uncommon statin-triggered syndrome; it tends to persist after statin cessation and has a longer subacute onset. Time-of-onset linkage to clarithromycin makes the interaction explanation more likely first. Source: ACR myopathy guidance.

options.text: Strenuous unrecognised exertional rhabdomyolysis
options.rationale: Exertional rhabdo would have a clear inciting episode in the days before symptom onset; the patient denies this and bilateral proximal distribution is unusual. Source: eTG Renal.

options.text: Perindopril-induced AKI causing secondary CK release
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors do not cause primary rhabdomyolysis; the CK 38 400 represents true muscle injury, not a creatinine artefact, and the AKI is secondary to myoglobin nephrotoxicity. Source: KDIGO AKI guidance.

explanation.summary: The discriminating cue is the timing of the macrolide course relative to symptom onset. Atorvastatin is metabolised by CYP3A4; clarithromycin is one of the strongest CYP3A4 inhibitors in clinical use. Co-administration produces dose-dependent rises in atorvastatin exposure and significantly increases rhabdomyolysis risk, with symptoms typically appearing within 5-14 days of macrolide initiation. Management: stop atorvastatin, IV crystalloid to maintain urine output >100 mL/h, monitor K and renal function, repeat CK trend. Restart a non-CYP3A4 statin (rosuvastatin, pravastatin) only once CK and renal function normalise.
key_points: Clarithromycin and erythromycin are strong CYP3A4 inhibitors
Atorvastatin, simvastatin, lovastatin are CYP3A4 substrates
Rosuvastatin and pravastatin are NOT CYP3A4 dependent
Statin-macrolide interaction is the single most common drug-induced rhabdomyolysis in AU primary care
Always pause CYP3A4-substrate statins for the duration of macrolide therapy
explanation.pearls: Helicobacter eradication regimens almost always include clarithromycin; flag statin-substrate switching at the time of prescribing.

subtopic_detail: Statin-macrolide CYP3A4 interaction rhabdomyolysis

stem:
A 78-year-old man on long-term warfarin for non-valvular AF (target INR 2.0-3.0) is admitted to Calvary Mater Newcastle with community-acquired pneumonia. He is in atrial fibrillation rate 102, BP 108/68, SpO2 92% on 2 L nasal prongs. His admission bloods show creatinine 198 micromol/L (baseline 90), urea 18 mmol/L, K 5.1, INR 7.4. He had a witnessed mechanical fall yesterday with a 2x2 cm forehead haematoma but no loss of consciousness; CT brain on arrival shows no intracranial haemorrhage.

He has no active bleeding now; urinalysis no blood. Past history: hypertension, mild Alzheimer dementia, prior PE 4 years ago. Medications: warfarin 5/4 mg alternate days, perindopril 5 mg, donepezil 10 mg. He weighs 72 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.2, HR 102 AF, BP 108/68, RR 24, SpO2 92% on 2 L NP.
data_table.Examination: Forehead haematoma 2x2 cm, no active bleed, no neurological deficit. Crepitations RLL. AF irregularly irregular.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 124, Plt 220, INR 7.4. Cr 198 (baseline 90), urea 18, K 5.1. CT brain no haemorrhage. CXR RLL consolidation.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step for management of his over-anticoagulation, integrating renal function, recent fall, and continued thromboembolic risk?

options.text: Withhold warfarin only and recheck INR in 24 hours; no other intervention
options.rationale: Withholding alone is appropriate for INR 4.5-10 in a stable patient without bleeding; this patient has multiple bleeding risk factors (recent fall with extracranial haematoma, AKI raising drug-handling unpredictability, age, dementia) that push the threshold for active reversal. Source: BSH Anticoagulation Guidelines; eTG Cardiovascular.

options.text: Stop warfarin and give oral phytomenadione (vitamin K1) 1-2 mg PO once
options.rationale: INR 4.5-10 without bleeding but with elevated bleeding risk (recent head trauma + extracranial haematoma + AKI + age) is reversed with low-dose oral vitamin K1 1-2 mg, plus warfarin withhold and INR recheck at 12-24 hours. IV vitamin K reserved for major bleeding (not present here despite negative CT). PCC is for major bleeding. Continued AF anticoagulation is reassessed after the acute illness; not stopped permanently. Source: eTG Cardiovascular; AHA AF Guidelines; ANZSBT/NBA Massive Transfusion / Warfarin reversal flowchart.

options.text: Stop warfarin, give 4-factor PCC 50 IU/kg plus IV vitamin K1 10 mg
options.rationale: Prothrombinex-VF (Australian 3-factor PCC) plus IV vitamin K1 is for major or life-threatening bleeding, not for non-bleeding over-anticoagulation. CT brain is clear; haematoma is extracranial and not active. This is over-reversal and risks rebound thrombosis. Source: ANZSBT.

options.text: Substitute warfarin with therapeutic enoxaparin 1 mg/kg BD bridging
options.rationale: Enoxaparin at 1 mg/kg BD is contraindicated at this creatinine clearance (estimated <30 mL/min): use renally-dose-reduced enoxaparin (1 mg/kg daily) or UFH if bridging needed - and bridging itself is rarely indicated for AF cessation of <1 week. The over-anticoagulation should be reversed first, not switched. Source: AMH; AHA AF Guidelines.

options.text: Continue warfarin at full dose; treat pneumonia and reassess INR after antibiotics complete
options.rationale: Continuing supratherapeutic warfarin in a patient with AKI, recent head trauma and infection is unsafe; INR will likely climb further as antibiotics displace warfarin from protein binding and inhibit gut vitamin K synthesis. Source: eTG Cardiovascular.

explanation.summary: Warfarin reversal sits on a ladder: withhold (INR 4.5-10 stable, no risk factors) → oral vitamin K1 1-2 mg (INR 4.5-10 with bleeding risk factors OR INR >10 stable) → IV vitamin K1 5-10 mg + PCC (major or life-threatening bleeding). This patient is at INR 7.4 without active bleeding but has multiple amplifiers of bleeding risk (recent fall with extracranial haematoma, age, dementia, AKI affecting drug clearance and platelet function). That moves him from 'withhold alone' to 'low-dose vitamin K1', without justifying full PCC reversal. AKI also matters for any LMWH bridging decision: enoxaparin needs dose reduction at CrCl <30 mL/min. Long-term, anticoagulation will be reassessed after acute illness resolves; falls themselves are not an absolute contraindication if HAS-BLED-adjusted benefit favours continuation.
key_points: INR 4.5-10, no bleeding, no risk factors: withhold alone
INR 4.5-10, no bleeding, but bleeding risk factors: add oral vitamin K1 1-2 mg
INR >10 or any major bleed: IV vitamin K1 5-10 mg + Prothrombinex-VF
Renal impairment: enoxaparin at CrCl <30 mL/min needs dose reduction or switch to UFH
AKI also impairs warfarin metabolism and platelet function - lower threshold for reversal
explanation.pearls: Antibiotics that potentiate warfarin: ciprofloxacin, metronidazole, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, macrolides, azoles. Anticipate INR rise and pre-emptively reduce warfarin dose.

subtopic_detail: Warfarin reversal ladder in multi-organ dysfunction

stem:
A 16-year-old boy presents to a Tamworth paediatric ED with 24 hours of mild central chest discomfort, no syncope, no palpitations, no exertional symptoms. Exam normal, no pericardial rub, no murmur. ECG sinus rhythm, no ST changes, no PR depression. Troponin I 87 ng/L (upper limit of normal 14). He received his second dose of an mRNA COVID-19 booster 48 hours ago.

He has no past cardiac history, no family history of cardiomyopathy or sudden death, no recent viral illness, no drug use, no stimulant medications. Echocardiogram requested.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8, HR 78, BP 118/72, SpO2 99% room air.
data_table.Examination: Heart sounds normal, no rub, no murmur, no peripheral oedema, chest non-tender.
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus normal axis, no ischaemia or pericarditis. Troponin I 87 ng/L (ULN 14). CRP 12. FBC normal. Echocardiogram pending.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge with safety-netting; troponin rise is fully explained by recent vaccination and requires no investigation
options.rationale: While post-mRNA-vaccine myocarditis is the leading differential, any troponin rise warrants echocardiography and cardiology review before discharge to exclude reduced LVEF or wall-motion abnormality. Source: NSW Health post-vaccine myocarditis guidance.

options.text: Admit for telemetry, complete echocardiography, repeat troponin at 6 hours, paediatric cardiology review, and recognise post-mRNA-vaccine myocarditis as the most likely cause
options.rationale: Post-mRNA vaccine myocarditis is the most likely cause (young male, 1-5 days post second/booster dose, chest pain with troponin rise, normal pre-vaccine cardiac state). Confound elimination: ECG and FH excluded primary cardiomyopathy and ischaemic causes. Standard pathway is admission with telemetry, echo, repeat troponin, paediatric cardiology review. Course is usually self-limiting with NSAIDs or low-dose colchicine but ATAGI/NSW Health pathway is mandatory. Source: NSW Health Post-Vaccine Myocarditis Pathway; ATAGI Clinical Recommendations.

options.text: Commence empirical aspirin 300 mg orally and admit as suspected acute coronary syndrome
options.rationale: ACS in a 16-year-old with no risk factors and a normal ECG is highly unlikely; aspirin loading is inappropriate. Source: eTG Cardiovascular.

options.text: Order CT coronary angiogram before any other intervention
options.rationale: CTCA is not first-line in adolescent myocarditis workup; echocardiography is. Cardiac MRI is the gold standard for post-vaccine myocarditis confirmation but is not the immediate next step in ED. Source: NSW Health Pathway.

options.text: Reassure and arrange outpatient stress test in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Outpatient deferral fails to exclude active myocarditis in the acute window when echocardiographic findings (segmental wall-motion abnormality, mild LVEF reduction) determine inpatient versus outpatient management. Source: NSW Health Pathway.

explanation.summary: The discriminator is recognising the confound: recent mRNA vaccine (24-72 h after second/booster dose, predominantly adolescent and young adult males) is the dominant cause of acute myopericarditis in this demographic since 2021. The reasoning trap is treating it as ACS or dismissing the troponin altogether. Standard Australian pathway is admit + telemetry + echo + repeat troponin + cardiology consult; cardiac MRI in 4-12 weeks confirms diagnosis. ATAGI maintains COVID-19 vaccine recommendations because benefit exceeds risk even when myocarditis is counted.
key_points: Post-mRNA-vaccine myocarditis: 1-5 days post 2nd/booster, peaks day 2-3
Male adolescents and young adults at highest relative risk
Always exclude reduced LVEF before outpatient management
Cardiac MRI at 4-12 weeks confirms scar/oedema pattern
ATAGI recommends a temporary 6-12 month pause on further mRNA doses in confirmed cases
explanation.pearls: Differentiating cause-and-effect from confound is a recurring exam skill: post-transfusion bilirubin rise, post-vaccine serology positives, post-surgery troponin rise, post-statin CK rise. Always state the alternative diagnoses you excluded.

subtopic_detail: Post-mRNA-vaccine myocarditis vs alternative myocardial injury

stem:
A 31-year-old woman is referred to a maternal medicine clinic at the Calvary Mater Newcastle at 16 weeks gestation with a known diagnosis of Sjogren syndrome made 4 years ago. She is asymptomatic, on hydroxychloroquine 200 mg daily, and has had no thrombotic events. Autoantibody profile from her rheumatologist 6 months ago: ANA 1:1280 speckled, anti-Ro (SSA) strongly positive at 240 U/mL, anti-La (SSB) positive at 90 U/mL, anti-dsDNA negative, anticardiolipin negative, lupus anticoagulant negative, anti-Smith negative, anti-RNP negative.

Her previous pregnancy 3 years ago ended in elective termination at 9 weeks. She has no children. She is on no other medications and has no thyroid disease.

data_table.Examination: No active synovitis, no rash, mild dry eyes only.
data_table.Investigations: ANA 1:1280 speckled, anti-Ro (SSA) 240 U/mL strongly positive, anti-La (SSB) 90 U/mL positive, dsDNA neg, anticardiolipin neg, LA neg. TFT normal.

lead_in: Based on the antibody pattern, which is the single most important antenatal surveillance step beyond standard screening?

options.text: Serial uterine artery Doppler at 20 and 24 weeks for placental insufficiency risk
options.rationale: Uterine artery Doppler is for placental insufficiency / preeclampsia risk stratification and is non-specific for this serology pattern. The dominant foetal risk in anti-Ro positive pregnancies is congenital heart block, not placental insufficiency. Source: RANZCOG.

options.text: Weekly foetal echocardiography focused on PR interval from 16 to 26 weeks
options.rationale: The discriminator is the anti-Ro (SSA) titre. Anti-Ro IgG crosses the placenta and binds foetal cardiac conduction tissue, producing congenital heart block (CHB) - irreversible once third-degree. Window of highest risk is 16-26 weeks gestation. AU practice: weekly foetal echo with PR-interval measurement; first-degree block may be reversible with maternal dexamethasone. Risk in first anti-Ro pregnancy ~1-2 percent; recurrence in subsequent affected pregnancy ~15-20 percent. Source: RANZCOG; AHA Foetal Cardiology Statement; ACR Reproductive Health Guideline.

options.text: Monthly maternal serum complement (C3 / C4) levels for lupus flare detection
options.rationale: Complement monitoring is used in SLE for flare surveillance but is non-specific for Sjogren and does not address the foetal risk. Source: ACR.

options.text: Maternal aspirin 150 mg from 12 weeks for preeclampsia prevention
options.rationale: Aspirin prophylaxis is appropriate per general preeclampsia risk algorithm but is not the most important step specific to this antibody pattern. It does not prevent congenital heart block. Source: RANZCOG.

options.text: Serial foetal MRI from 20 weeks for neural-tube and brain anomalies
options.rationale: Foetal MRI is not part of routine surveillance for anti-Ro positive pregnancies and the antibodies do not cause CNS malformation. Source: RANZCOG.

explanation.summary: Anti-Ro and anti-La are IgG autoantibodies present in Sjogren syndrome, SLE, and isolated asymptomatic seropositivity. They cross the placenta from approximately 16 weeks gestation and can bind to the foetal AV node, producing inflammatory damage and progressive heart block. First-degree block may respond to maternal dexamethasone or fluorinated steroids; second-degree is sometimes reversible; third-degree is irreversible and may require foetal/neonatal pacing. Weekly foetal echo with PR-interval (mechanical) assessment 16-26 weeks is the AU surveillance standard for anti-Ro positive pregnancies. Neonatal lupus erythematosus (skin rash, hepatitis, cytopenias) is the postnatal counterpart and resolves as maternal IgG is cleared (~6 months).
key_points: Anti-Ro/SSA + anti-La/SSB cross placenta from ~16 weeks
Surveillance window: 16-26 weeks for congenital heart block
Continue hydroxychloroquine in pregnancy (compatible, reduces CHB risk)
First-degree block may respond to maternal dexamethasone
Recurrence risk of CHB in subsequent affected pregnancy ~15-20 percent
explanation.pearls: Hydroxychloroquine is associated with reduced incidence of congenital heart block in anti-Ro positive pregnancies (PATCH trial); continuation through pregnancy is supported by RANZCOG.

subtopic_detail: Anti-Ro associated congenital heart block surveillance

stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 woman is admitted to John Hunter Hospital at 29+5 weeks gestation in established preterm labour. Birth is anticipated within 6 hours. She has received the first dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM 30 minutes ago. The obstetric registrar is preparing to commence an infusion of a magnesium-based agent whose primary intended foetal effect is described as: 'a calcium-channel modulator that stabilises immature cerebral microvasculature, attenuates glutamate-driven excitotoxicity at NMDA receptors, and reduces free-radical-mediated white matter injury in the periventricular region.' Maternal observations are normal, no preeclampsia features, no contraindications to magnesium therapy.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7, HR 88, BP 118/72, RR 18, SpO2 99% room air. Patellar reflexes 2+.
data_table.Examination: Cervix 4 cm dilated, 80% effaced, cephalic, station -1; CTG reactive.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC normal. Mg baseline 0.85 mmol/L. Patellar reflexes preserved.

lead_in: Based purely on the mechanism described, which is the single most likely clinical indication?

options.text: Eclampsia seizure prophylaxis in severe preeclampsia at term
options.rationale: Eclampsia prophylaxis is indeed a magnesium indication but acts via maternal cortical neuronal stabilisation, not foetal periventricular white-matter protection. The mechanism described targets the immature foetal brain specifically. Source: RANZCOG.

options.text: Tocolysis to delay preterm birth by 48 hours for steroid effect
options.rationale: Magnesium has been used historically as a tocolytic but is no longer recommended in Australia (no benefit over placebo). The mechanism described is neuroprotective, not myometrial. Source: RANZCOG Preterm Labour Guidance.

options.text: Foetal neuroprotection from cerebral palsy in anticipated preterm birth under 30 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Australian Antenatal Magnesium sulfate for Neuroprotection Clinical Practice Guideline (2010, reaffirmed) recommends IV magnesium sulfate for women at <30 weeks gestation with anticipated birth within 24 hours, to reduce cerebral palsy risk in surviving infants. Mechanism is calcium-channel modulation + glutamate/NMDA attenuation + free-radical scavenging at the developing periventricular white matter (which is highly vulnerable to hypoxic injury). NNT ~63 to prevent one case of CP. Loading 4 g IV over 20 min then 1 g/h until birth or 24 h. Source: Australian Antenatal Magnesium sulfate Neuroprotection Guideline; RANZCOG.
source_refs: Australian Antenatal Magnesium Sulphate Neuroprotection Guideline

options.text: Maternal treatment of supraventricular tachycardia in pregnancy
options.rationale: Magnesium has class effect at ion channels but is not used as primary SVT therapy; mechanism described is neuro-specific. Source: AHA.

options.text: Maternal treatment of acute severe asthma with bronchodilator effect
options.rationale: IV magnesium has a role in severe asthma via smooth muscle relaxation, but the mechanism described targets foetal periventricular tissue, not adult bronchial smooth muscle. Source: TSANZ.

explanation.summary: MgSO4 for foetal neuroprotection acts via voltage-gated calcium channel modulation, NMDA receptor antagonism, and free-radical scavenging in the immature periventricular white matter, which is particularly vulnerable to hypoxic-ischaemic and inflammatory injury before 30 weeks gestation. Australian practice: indicated when birth is anticipated within 24 hours and gestation is <30 weeks (some units extend to 32 weeks). Maternal magnesium toxicity is monitored by patellar reflexes (loss at >3.5 mmol/L), respiratory rate, and urine output. Calcium gluconate IV is the antidote.
key_points: AU indication: anticipated birth <30 weeks within 24 hours
Dose: 4 g IV over 20 min loading, then 1 g/h
NNT ~63 to prevent one case of cerebral palsy
Monitor patellar reflexes hourly; RR; urine output
Calcium gluconate IV reverses toxicity
explanation.pearls: Same drug, different indication, different mechanism: eclampsia prophylaxis (maternal cortex), foetal neuroprotection (periventricular white matter), severe asthma (bronchodilation), torsades (membrane stabilisation). The mechanism described in the stem mapped uniquely to neuroprotection.

sources.label: Australian Antenatal Magnesium Sulphate Neuroprotection Guideline

subtopic_detail: Magnesium sulfate fetal neuroprotection mechanism vs other indications

stem:
A 34-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia has been stable on clozapine 450 mg nocte for 11 months, attending a Newcastle community mental health clinic. He smokes 20 cigarettes daily and has been advised against changing his smoking behaviour without consultation. He presents today with 4 days of intermittent sharp left-sided chest pain, mild dyspnoea on exertion (climbing one flight of stairs), and a single self-recorded resting heart rate of 122.

He denies fever, cough, sputum, syncope, or recent travel. His most recent FBC, UEC, LFT, and CK are within 7 days and normal except WCC 7.2 (with neutrophils 4.8) and CK 142 U/L. He has gained 8 kg over 11 months.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9, HR 118 sinus, BP 124/82, RR 22, SpO2 97% room air.
data_table.Examination: Heart sounds normal, no rub, mild bibasal fine crackles, no peripheral oedema, no calf tenderness, no rash.
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia 118, no ST changes. Troponin I 142 ng/L (ULN 14). NT-proBNP 380 pg/mL. CXR mild cardiomegaly. FBC: WCC 7.2 N 4.8; UEC normal; CK 142; LFT normal.

lead_in: Which clozapine-associated adverse effect is the single most likely explanation for this presentation, and what is the immediate monitoring response?

options.text: Clozapine-induced agranulocytosis; cease clozapine and admit for haematology review
options.rationale: Neutrophil count is 4.8 (well above the 1.5 mandatory cessation threshold); agranulocytosis is not occurring. The presentation does not fit; agranulocytosis typically presents with sepsis from infection, not chest pain and troponin rise. Source: Australian Clozapine Monitoring Protocol.

options.text: Clozapine-induced myocarditis; cease clozapine, admit, urgent echocardiogram and cardiology consult
options.rationale: Clozapine-induced myocarditis occurs in 0.7-3 percent of patients, peaks 14-21 days after initiation but can occur later (especially with smoking-related metabolism changes). Presentation: tachycardia + chest pain + dyspnoea + raised troponin + raised CRP/NT-proBNP in a clozapine patient is myocarditis until proven otherwise. Mandatory cessation; cardiology review; echocardiogram for LVEF assessment; cardiac MRI for confirmation. Re-challenge generally not recommended. Source: RANZCP Clozapine Position Statement; Australian Clozapine Monitoring Protocol.

options.text: Clozapine-induced metabolic syndrome and weight gain; commence atorvastatin 40 mg daily and dietitian referral
options.rationale: Metabolic syndrome is a recognised clozapine effect but does not present acutely with chest pain and raised troponin in a young patient. The acute presentation demands cardiac evaluation. Source: RANZCP.

options.text: Clozapine-induced sialorrhoea; commence hyoscine hydrobromide 0.4 mg sublingually nocte
options.rationale: Sialorrhoea is a common clozapine side effect but is unrelated to acute cardiac presentation. Source: AMH.

options.text: Clozapine-induced ileus; nil orally, IV fluids, urgent abdominal X-ray
options.rationale: Gastrointestinal hypomotility is a serious clozapine effect but does not explain tachycardia + troponin + dyspnoea; the presentation is cardiac. Source: RANZCP.

explanation.summary: Clozapine's adverse-effect profile is broad and side-effect-specific monitoring schedules are mandatory in Australian practice. Mapping the presentation back to the side effect determines whether the drug must be stopped or continued. Acute chest pain + tachycardia + troponin elevation + dyspnoea in a clozapine patient is myocarditis until proven otherwise; mandatory cessation, cardiology consult, echocardiogram + cardiac MRI confirm. Other key clozapine adverse effects with their monitoring: agranulocytosis (FBC weekly 18 weeks then monthly), seizure (EEG/dose review at high doses), GI hypomotility (constipation history at each visit), metabolic syndrome (HbA1c + lipids 3-monthly first year), pulmonary embolism (high index of suspicion), sialorrhoea (clinical), hypotension (BP at each visit).
key_points: Clozapine myocarditis: peaks 14-21 days but possible later; mandatory cessation
Workup: ECG + troponin + CRP + NT-proBNP + echocardiogram + cardiac MRI
Smoking induces CYP1A2 - smoking cessation can double clozapine plasma level
Mandatory FBC monitoring: weekly x18 weeks, fortnightly x18 weeks, then monthly indefinitely
Re-challenge after myocarditis is not generally recommended in Australia
explanation.pearls: Smoking cessation in a clozapine patient is a pharmacokinetic event - plasma level can double within 1-2 weeks. Always consult before any smoking change, including admission to a non-smoking ward.

subtopic_detail: Clozapine myocarditis recognition + mandatory cessation

stem:
A 31-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder presents to a Gosford perinatal mental health clinic at 7 weeks gestation. Her bipolar history includes two manic episodes requiring hospitalisation 5 and 8 years ago, three depressive episodes between, and one suicide attempt by overdose 3 years ago. She has been euthymic for 18 months on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily as monotherapy.

She has now stopped valproate on her own initiative 2 weeks ago after discovering the pregnancy. She has a personal history of acne treated with isotretinoin 8 years ago (now resolved), no thyroid disease, normal renal function (Cr 65), and a strong family history of bipolar disorder in her mother. She is keen to continue the pregnancy and plans to breastfeed. She has a supportive partner.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic, no psychotic symptoms, MSE unremarkable, judgment intact.
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.8 mIU/L, free T4 normal. Cr 65 micromol/L. LFT normal. Beta-hCG confirms 7-week pregnancy.

lead_in: Which mood stabiliser strategy best satisfies ALL the following constraints: minimal teratogenic risk, established efficacy in bipolar maintenance, compatibility with breastfeeding, and minimum complexity of monitoring?

options.text: Restart sodium valproate at half-dose with 5 mg folate; the second trimester is past the highest teratogenicity window
options.rationale: Valproate is contraindicated in pregnancy at any gestation (neural-tube defects, autism, lower IQ) and is single-strongest absolute contraindication in the Australian Pregnancy Category D listings for bipolar. Restarting is wrong. Source: RANZCP Perinatal Position Statement; AMH.

options.text: Quetiapine 300-600 mg daily as monotherapy; trimester-specific dose reviews; compatible with breastfeeding
options.rationale: Quetiapine satisfies all four constraints: teratogenic risk is among the lowest of mood stabilisers/atypical antipsychotics in pregnancy (Pregnancy Category B3), has good evidence for bipolar maintenance, is breastfeeding-compatible (low RID typically <1 percent), and requires minimal additional monitoring beyond standard antenatal. Lithium is more efficacious but requires intensive monitoring (TFT, renal, lithium level), 12-week foetal echo for Ebstein anomaly, and is excreted in breast milk at significant levels. Source: RANZCP Perinatal Position Statement; Bipolar Disorder Australian Treatment Guide; LactMed.

options.text: Lithium 750 mg daily; weekly levels first trimester; 12-week and 20-week foetal echo for Ebstein anomaly; cease at 6 weeks postpartum to enable breastfeeding
options.rationale: Lithium is highly effective and reasonable in pregnancy if antenatal monitoring is feasible, but the question asks for the strategy that minimises monitoring complexity AND maintains breastfeeding compatibility - lithium requires intensive monitoring and is not first-line for breastfeeding women (high RID, neonatal toxicity risk). Source: RANZCP.

options.text: Carbamazepine 600 mg daily; 5 mg folate daily; vitamin K 20 mg from 36 weeks to prevent neonatal haemorrhage
options.rationale: Carbamazepine is teratogenic (NTD, craniofacial defects) and is no longer recommended for bipolar in women of reproductive age in Australian guidance. Source: RANZCP; AMH.

options.text: Cease all mood stabilisers; weekly psychiatry review; restart only if relapse occurs
options.rationale: Wait-and-see strategy in a patient with two prior manic admissions and one suicide attempt is unsafe - relapse risk during pregnancy off all maintenance approaches 80 percent in 6 months. Source: RANZCP.

explanation.summary: Constraint stacking forces the candidate to weigh teratogenicity (favours lamotrigine, quetiapine over valproate, carbamazepine, lithium), efficacy in bipolar maintenance (favours lithium, valproate, quetiapine, lamotrigine, olanzapine), breastfeeding compatibility (favours lamotrigine, quetiapine, sertraline, olanzapine; avoid lithium, valproate, carbamazepine), and monitoring complexity (favours quetiapine, lamotrigine over lithium). Quetiapine satisfies all four; lamotrigine is reasonable for bipolar depression maintenance but less effective for mania prevention. Patient autonomy and shared decision-making frame the discussion: each option's evidence and trade-off should be discussed explicitly.
key_points: Valproate: absolutely avoid in women of reproductive age unless no alternative
Lithium: most efficacious but intensive monitoring; not first-line in BF
Quetiapine: best balance of efficacy, safety, BF compatibility, simplicity
Lamotrigine: best for bipolar depression maintenance; titration risk of SJS/TEN
Sudden discontinuation of mood stabiliser in pregnancy: relapse risk approaches 80% in 6 months
explanation.pearls: When discussing pregnancy planning in any woman of reproductive age on valproate, document the discussion explicitly - PBS access in Australia now requires a pregnancy-prevention attestation.

subtopic_detail: Mood stabiliser selection under multiple pregnancy/BF constraints

stem:
A 4-year-old previously well girl is brought to Maitland Hospital paediatric ED with 5 days of bloody diarrhoea, 24 hours of pallor, and 12 hours of reduced urine output. Her family returned 9 days ago from a 3-week farm-stay holiday on the NSW mid-north coast where the children regularly fed unpasteurised milk to orphan calves. Initial GP-ordered stool PCR is pending.

Today she appears pale, irritable, with abdominal tenderness but no peritonism. She has had no antibiotic exposure for this illness. There is no rash, no jaundice, no recent vaccinations, no medication exposure. Family history is unremarkable for renal or haematological disease.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4, HR 138, BP 105/72 (above the 95th centile for age), RR 26, SpO2 98%, weight 16 kg, urine output 0.4 mL/kg/h over 12 h.
data_table.Examination: Pallor, mild scleral icterus, generalised abdominal tenderness, no peritonism, no organomegaly, no rash, no peripheral oedema (yet).
data_table.Investigations: Hb 76 g/L, platelets 42 x10^9/L, WCC 14, blood film: schistocytes ++ and helmet cells. Reticulocytes elevated. Cr 168 micromol/L (baseline unknown, eGFR markedly reduced for age). LDH 1840 U/L, haptoglobin undetectable, INR/aPTT normal, Coombs negative. Bilirubin 38 micromol/L (mostly unconjugated). Stool PCR pending.

lead_in: Which is the single most likely diagnosis, and which clinical action is contraindicated?

options.text: Immune thrombocytopaenic purpura; commence prednisolone 2 mg/kg orally
options.rationale: ITP causes isolated thrombocytopenia, not the microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia + AKI triad described. Schistocytes on film exclude ITP. Source: RCH CPG.

options.text: STEC-associated haemolytic uraemic syndrome (D+HUS); supportive management and avoid empirical antibiotics
options.rationale: Pattern: bloody diarrhoea + microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (schistocytes, low haptoglobin, raised LDH, raised unconjugated bilirubin, Coombs negative) + thrombocytopaenia + AKI = classic STEC-HUS. Non-obvious cue is unpasteurised milk + calf contact at a farm-stay (STEC O157 reservoirs include cattle and unpasteurised dairy in Australia; multiple outbreaks). CONTRAINDICATED: empirical antibiotics (associated with increased Shiga toxin release and worsened HUS in some studies), antimotility agents, NSAIDs, platelet transfusion (paradoxical worsening). Management is supportive (careful IV fluids, monitoring for dialysis indication, blood transfusion if Hb critically low, plasma exchange in atypical HUS only). Eculizumab for atypical HUS, not STEC-HUS. Source: RCH CPG: HUS; eTG Paediatrics; CDNA STEC SoNG.

options.text: Acute leukaemia with disseminated intravascular coagulation; commence empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics
options.rationale: Coagulation profile is normal (INR/aPTT normal), excluding DIC. WCC is mildly elevated but no blast cells. Source: RCH CPG.

options.text: Acute glomerulonephritis post-infectious; commence furosemide and start ACE inhibitor
options.rationale: Post-infectious GN typically presents with hypertension, oedema, haematuria, mild proteinuria - not the microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia pattern. ACE inhibitor in AKI worsens renal function. Source: RCH CPG.

options.text: Autoimmune haemolytic anaemia; commence prednisolone 2 mg/kg and IVIG
options.rationale: Coombs is NEGATIVE - rules out warm or cold AIHA. Pattern is microangiopathic (schistocytes, MAHA), not autoimmune. Source: RCH CPG.

explanation.summary: STEC-HUS (formerly D+HUS) is the dominant paediatric cause of acute kidney injury in Australia in children under 5. Triad: microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopaenia, AKI - usually 5-10 days after a prodrome of bloody diarrhoea. Mechanism: Shiga toxin from E. coli O157 (and other STEC strains) binds Gb3 glycosphingolipid on glomerular endothelium → endothelial swelling → microthrombi → mechanical RBC shear and platelet consumption. Antibiotics are contraindicated because Shiga toxin release is upregulated by bacterial stress response (multiple RCT-level signals of harm). Supportive care, blood transfusion when needed, dialysis if oligoanuric or hyperkalaemic. Notifiable to NSW Health public health unit.
key_points: Triad: MAHA + thrombocytopaenia + AKI after bloody diarrhoea prodrome
STEC sources: unpasteurised dairy, ground beef, petting farms, contaminated water
Antibiotics CONTRAINDICATED in suspected STEC-HUS
NSAIDs, antimotility agents, platelet transfusion all contraindicated
Notifiable disease - inform NSW Health public health unit
explanation.pearls: Atypical HUS (complement-mediated) presents without diarrhoea prodrome and is treated with eculizumab; STEC-HUS is supportive only. Always ask about farm visits, raw milk, and recent ground-beef consumption in paediatric AKI workup.

subtopic_detail: STEC-HUS pattern recognition + antibiotic contraindication

stem:
A 3-year-old boy weighing 14 kg is triaged at John Hunter Hospital paediatric ED at 0220 with 18 hours of fever, lethargy and decreased oral intake. Parents describe a non-blanching rash that developed in the last hour on both legs. He has not been immunised. Initial assessment: T 39.4, HR 178, RR 44, capillary refill 4 seconds peripherally and 3 seconds centrally, mottled extremities, BP 88/52, SpO2 96% room air.

He is drowsy but rousable, no neck stiffness on initial exam but obtunded enough to limit reliable testing. Blood glucose 5.4 mmol/L. The triage nurse is initiating the NSW Health Paediatric Sepsis Pathway.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.4 deg C, HR 178, RR 44, BP 88/52, SpO2 96% RA, CRT 4 sec peripheral, weight 14 kg.
data_table.Examination: Lethargic but rousable, mottled extremities, non-blanching petechial rash both legs, dry mucous membranes, no obvious focus.
data_table.Investigations: Initial venous gas pending; bedside lactate 4.8 mmol/L; FBC, UEC, CRP, blood cultures, meningococcal PCR all sent.

lead_in: Within the NSW Health Paediatric Sepsis Pathway, what is the most appropriate fluid resuscitation and antibiotic decision for the first hour?

options.text: 10 mL/kg of 0.9% saline over 15 min and reassess; defer antibiotics until LP and CT brain
options.rationale: Septic-shock paediatric bolus per NSW Health is 20 mL/kg of 0.9% saline (or balanced crystalloid) given over 5-10 min and reassessed. Antibiotics must be given within the first hour - LP can be deferred (and is contraindicated in shock until stabilised). Source: NSW Health Paediatric Sepsis Pathway.

options.text: 20 mL/kg of 0.9% saline (280 mL) over 5-10 min, reassess, repeat to max 40-60 mL/kg with cardiovascular monitoring; ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg IV (max 2 g) within 60 min of recognition; consider early inotropes if no response to second bolus
options.rationale: NSW Health Paediatric Sepsis Pathway: 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus over 5-10 min, repeat to max 40-60 mL/kg watching for fluid-overload signs (hepatomegaly, increased work of breathing) before considering inotropes. Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) IV is first-line empirical (meningococcal cover). Antibiotic must be administered within 60 min of sepsis recognition. Early inotrope (adrenaline 0.1-0.2 mcg/kg/min) if no response to second bolus. Lactate >4 = severe sepsis criterion. Source: NSW Health Paediatric Sepsis Pathway; CHOPP guideline.

options.text: 40 mL/kg of 4% albumin over 30 min as initial bolus to address capillary leak; vancomycin and meropenem within 60 min
options.rationale: Crystalloid (not colloid) is first-line in paediatric sepsis (FEAST trial impact + Australian preference). 40 mL/kg as a single push is contraindicated - boluses are 20 mL/kg with reassessment between each. Vancomycin + meropenem is overkill for community-acquired sepsis without specific MRSA/multi-resistant risk. Source: NSW Health Pathway.

options.text: Defer fluid resuscitation; commence adrenaline infusion 0.5 mcg/kg/min immediately as primary therapy
options.rationale: Fluid resuscitation precedes inotropes in compensated paediatric septic shock. Adrenaline is added after 2 boluses fail to restore perfusion, not as primary therapy. Source: NSW Pathway.

options.text: 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline over 60 min; benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg IV; arrange immediate LP to confirm meningitis
options.rationale: Bolus over 60 min is too slow for resuscitation (target 5-10 min). LP in shock is contraindicated until cardiovascular stability. Benzylpenicillin monotherapy is too narrow for empirical adult septic shock cover in current AU practice. Source: NSW Pathway; eTG.

explanation.summary: The discriminator is recall of the NSW Health Paediatric Sepsis Pathway specific cut-offs: bolus volume 20 mL/kg over 5-10 min (not 10, not 40), antibiotic within 60 min, max fluid 40-60 mL/kg before inotrope consideration, ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) as first-line. The clinical picture (compensated septic shock with petechial rash, unimmunised) strongly suggests meningococcaemia; meningococcal PCR confirms but does not delay treatment. Public health notification within 24 hours.
key_points: NSW paeds septic shock bolus: 20 mL/kg crystalloid over 5-10 min, reassess each
Max ~40-60 mL/kg before inotropes (watch for fluid overload)
First antibiotic within 60 min of recognition
Empirical: ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) IV in community-acquired sepsis
Lactate >4 + tachycardia + mottled = severe sepsis criteria
explanation.pearls: LP is contraindicated in shock until cardiovascular stability is restored. Empirical antibiotics first; LP later. Notify NSW Health public health unit for meningococcal.

subtopic_detail: NSW Health paediatric sepsis pathway cut-offs

stem:
A 32-year-old G2P2 woman is reviewed in Calvary Mater Newcastle maternity ward day 4 post elective lower-segment caesarean section for breech at term. She has a temperature of 38.6 degrees Celsius, mild lower abdominal tenderness, no offensive lochia, and breastfeeding well. Pre-operative antibiotic prophylaxis was cefazolin. Wound site is dry without erythema. There is no calf swelling and no respiratory symptoms.

Urinalysis is negative. Foley catheter was removed day 2. Her temperature has now been over 38 degrees for 12 hours despite paracetamol. Blood pressure 118/76, heart rate 96 sinus, respiratory rate 18, SpO2 98 percent.

data_table.Examination: Mild lower abdominal tenderness without peritonism; uterus appropriate involution, lochia rubra without offensive odour; wound clean; calves soft, no swelling; chest clear.
data_table.Investigations: FBC: WCC 14.2 x10^9/L; CRP 88 mg/L. UEC normal. Urinalysis negative. Blood cultures sent. Pelvic USS pending.

lead_in: Based on the timing relative to delivery and the constellation of negative findings, which is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Wound infection at the caesarean operative site with cellulitis
options.rationale: Wound is dry without erythema or discharge; wound infections typically present day 4-7 with localised signs that are absent here. Source: RANZCOG; eTG Antibiotic.

options.text: Endometritis from ascending genital tract organisms after caesarean
options.rationale: TIME-OF-ONSET discriminator: endometritis is the most common cause of puerperal sepsis at day 2-10 post-caesarean (risk 5-10x vaginal birth even with prophylaxis). Diagnostic constellation: fever >38, uterine tenderness, raised WCC + CRP, lochia may or may not be offensive (often subtle), other foci negative (wound, urine, chest, DVT). Treatment: gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV daily + ampicillin 2 g IV 6-hourly + metronidazole 500 mg IV 12-hourly (or as per local NSW Health antibiotic stewardship). Source: RANZCOG Postnatal Infections; eTG Antibiotic; NSW Health Maternity Sepsis.

options.text: Mastitis from ongoing breastfeeding with milk stasis
options.rationale: Mastitis would typically present with breast pain, erythema and induration; chest findings absent. Source: ABA; eTG.

options.text: Deep vein thrombosis from postpartum hypercoagulable state
options.rationale: DVT presents with calf swelling, tenderness, asymmetry; calf exam negative. DVT also less commonly causes fever this high. Pulmonary embolism would present with tachycardia + dyspnoea + hypoxia. Source: RANZCOG VTE in Pregnancy.

options.text: Urinary tract infection following recent indwelling catheter use
options.rationale: Urinalysis is negative; catheter was removed day 2 (48 hours before symptom onset). Possible but less likely than endometritis given the timing and uterine tenderness. Source: eTG Antibiotic.

explanation.summary: Puerperal fever (>38 degrees within 14 days postpartum) requires systematic differential by timing and physical findings: day 1-2 favours atelectasis or wound; day 2-10 favours endometritis (especially post-caesarean); day 7-14 favours wound infection, mastitis, DVT/PE; any time UTI, catheter-related infection. Endometritis incidence is ~3 percent of vaginal births but ~5-10x higher with caesarean even after prophylaxis. Triple antibiotic regimen (ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole) covers polymicrobial flora typical of endometritis (anaerobes, group B strep, E coli, enterococcus, gardnerella). Resolution typically within 48 hours; persistent fever suggests abscess, retained products, or pelvic vein thrombophlebitis - imaging indicated.
key_points: Puerperal fever day 2-10: endometritis top of differential
Caesarean increases risk 5-10x vs vaginal even with prophylaxis
AU triple therapy: ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole
Persistent fever despite 48h appropriate antibiotics: imaging for abscess
Postpartum VTE risk persists 6 weeks; high index of suspicion
explanation.pearls: Septic pelvic thrombophlebitis is the diagnosis of exclusion in persistent puerperal fever with normal imaging - empirical heparin produces dramatic defervescence within 48 hours and confirms diagnosis.

subtopic_detail: Puerperal fever timing-based differential

stem:
A 22-year-old woman with severe restricting-type anorexia nervosa over 5 years is brought to John Hunter Hospital ED by her mother. The patient is refusing admission. She weighs 38 kg (height 168 cm, BMI 13.5), heart rate 38 bpm sinus, BP 78/48 supine and 65/40 standing, T 35.2 degrees Celsius, glucose 2.8 mmol/L, K 2.6 mmol/L, P 0.42 mmol/L, Mg 0.62 mmol/L.

ECG shows sinus bradycardia with QTc 510 ms. She is alert, coherent, and capable of stating she understands she could die but does not want medical treatment because she fears weight restoration. She has no other psychiatric comorbidity and no acute psychosis. Her mother has lasting power of attorney for property only.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 38 sinus, BP 78/48 supine to 65/40 standing, T 35.2 deg C, weight 38 kg (BMI 13.5), SpO2 95%.
data_table.Examination: Cachexia, lanugo hair, dry mucous membranes, peripheral cyanosis, alert and oriented, MSE no thought disorder, insight present for medical consequences but absent for need for treatment.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 2.8 mmol/L, K 2.6 mmol/L, P 0.42 mmol/L (low), Mg 0.62 mmol/L (low), ECG sinus brady 38 with QTc 510 ms. Cr 75, urea 4.

lead_in: Which combination of MARSIPAN-AU admission criteria and NSW Mental Health Act 2007 framework most appropriately governs her treatment, given that she is refusing care?

options.text: Discharge against advice with daily outpatient follow-up; her capacity is intact so no involuntary admission is possible
options.rationale: MARSIPAN-AU criteria for mandatory medical admission are met (BMI <14, HR <40, postural BP drop >20 mmHg systolic, T <35.5, glucose <3, K <2.5 [borderline], QTc >500 ms). Discharge would be negligent regardless of stated capacity, because severe AN classically presents with intact superficial reasoning but a circumscribed inability to weight-restore. NSW MHA Section 14 mental illness criteria can be met for severe AN where the eating disorder substantially impairs judgment. Source: MARSIPAN-AU; NSW MHA 2007.

options.text: Admit to a medical ward under NSW MHA 2007 Schedule 1 (involuntary mental health assessment) with refeeding under specialist eating-disorder protocol; commence oral phosphate, low-dose IV thiamine 300 mg BD, refeed at 5-10 kcal/kg/day initial rate; daily P/Mg/K; cardiac monitoring for QTc and bradycardia
options.rationale: Combined trigger: ALL MARSIPAN-AU admission criteria are met (BMI 13.5, HR 38, postural BP drop, T 35.2, glucose 2.8, QTc 510).
