Under NSW MHA 2007, severe AN with refusal of care meets Schedule 1 criteria when the eating disorder is judged to substantially impair the patient's ability to make safe treatment decisions about feeding/weight restoration. Refeeding starts low (5-10 kcal/kg/day; progress per protocol over 5-7 days) to prevent refeeding syndrome; phosphate and thiamine pre-treatment are mandatory; cardiac monitoring for QTc and arrhythmia. Family is consulted; the eating-disorder service is involved early. Source: MARSIPAN-AU; NSW MHA 2007; RANZCP Eating Disorders Position.

options.text: Admit voluntarily and commence standard adult enteral nutrition at 30 kcal/kg/day via nasogastric tube to rapidly restore weight
options.rationale: Starting at 30 kcal/kg/day in severe AN triggers refeeding syndrome (intracellular phosphate/magnesium/potassium shift causing arrhythmia, cardiomyopathy, encephalopathy). MARSIPAN-AU protocol starts at 5-10 kcal/kg/day and titrates. The patient is also refusing care - voluntary admission cannot be assumed. Source: MARSIPAN-AU.

options.text: Apply for guardianship through the NSW Civil and Administrative Tribunal (NCAT) for medical treatment authority; defer treatment until order is granted
options.rationale: Guardianship orders take days to weeks; her condition is acutely life-threatening. NSW MHA 2007 provides immediate involuntary admission authority for psychiatric emergencies. Guardianship may be a longer-term consideration. Source: NSW MHA 2007.

options.text: Sedate with IM midazolam 5 mg, intubate for airway protection, commence IV TPN 25 kcal/kg/day with continuous insulin infusion to manage hyperglycaemia
options.rationale: Sedation and intubation are inappropriate in an alert cooperative-to-discussion patient. TPN is reserved for failed enteral nutrition; oral or NG is preferred. Rate of 25 kcal/kg/day day 1 risks refeeding syndrome. Source: MARSIPAN-AU.

explanation.summary: Severe AN with medical instability + treatment refusal is one of the few situations where the medical and mental health systems intersect operationally. MARSIPAN-AU (Management of Really Sick Patients with Anorexia Nervosa - Australia/New Zealand) gives objective criteria for medical admission: BMI <14, HR <40, postural BP drop >20 mmHg, T <35.5, glucose <3, K <2.5, P <0.4, ECG QTc >500 or rhythm abnormality. When the patient refuses, NSW MHA 2007 Section 14 mental illness criteria (with eating disorder substantially impairing decision-making capacity around feeding) provides involuntary admission authority - even where surface capacity appears intact. Refeeding starts at 5-10 kcal/kg/day with pre-treatment phosphate + thiamine and progresses over 5-7 days under daily electrolyte monitoring. The principle: capacity to refuse weight restoration is not equivalent to capacity to refuse life-saving treatment for the underlying disorder.
key_points: MARSIPAN-AU admission triggers: BMI<14, HR<40, postural drop>20, T<35.5, gluc<3, K<2.5, QTc>500
NSW MHA 2007 Section 14 covers severe AN with treatment refusal
Refeed start: 5-10 kcal/kg/day, titrate over 5-7 days
Pre-treatment: phosphate + thiamine before any kcal
Cardiac monitoring mandatory: QTc, bradycardia, arrhythmia
explanation.pearls: Aboriginal Mental Health Worker referral is essential when the patient identifies as Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander; cultural-safety governance under NSW MHA 2007 includes consultation with the patient's preferred community supports.

subtopic_detail: MARSIPAN-AU admission threshold + NSW MHA 2007 framework

stem:
A 58-year-old man with bipolar I disorder on lithium carbonate 750 mg nocte for 12 years presents to Gosford ED with 5 days of nausea, coarse tremor, ataxia, slurred speech and increasing confusion. He started ramipril 5 mg 3 weeks ago for new hypertension. Three weeks ago he also started ibuprofen 400 mg TDS for chronic shoulder pain. He has had no fluid losses, no fever, no diuretics.

His last documented serum lithium 6 months ago was 0.7 mmol/L. Today: lithium 2.4 mmol/L, creatinine 138 micromol/L (baseline 84), Na 142, K 4.2, TSH normal, glucose 5.8. ECG sinus rhythm with U waves and prolonged QT.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8, HR 102 sinus, BP 142/88, RR 16, SpO2 98% RA, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6).
data_table.Examination: Coarse upper-limb tremor at rest, hyperreflexia 3+, ataxic gait, dysarthria, dysmetria, no focal weakness, confusion to recent events.
data_table.Investigations: Lithium 2.4 mmol/L (therapeutic 0.6-1.0), Cr 138 (baseline 84), Na 142, K 4.2, glucose 5.8. ECG sinus 102 with prolonged QT, U waves. TSH normal.

lead_in: Which is the single best explanation for the cerebellar and basal ganglia signs described, and which clinical scenario most predictably reproduces this mechanism?

options.text: Lithium interferes with neuronal sodium-potassium ATPase and Purkinje-cell function in cerebellum; recurs predictably when CYP450 inhibitors are added to the regimen
options.rationale: Lithium is not metabolised by CYP450 (it is purely renally excreted). CYP enzyme interactions are not the mechanism. The other half of the option (Na-K ATPase + Purkinje cell effect) is partially correct as a mechanism, but the predictability claim is wrong. Source: AMH; eTG Toxicology.

options.text: Lithium accumulates in neurons via secondary active transport substituting for sodium; clearance falls when GFR drops or proximal tubule sodium reabsorption increases (NSAID, ACEI, dehydration, low salt intake)
options.rationale: MECHANISM: lithium is freely filtered at the glomerulus and reabsorbed in the proximal tubule via sodium-coupled transporters. Anything that reduces GFR (NSAIDs via prostaglandin-mediated afferent arteriolar constriction, ACEIs via efferent arteriolar dilation) OR increases proximal sodium reabsorption (dehydration, low-salt diet, thiazides) causes lithium reabsorption to rise and serum level to climb. Cerebellar and basal ganglia neurons accumulate lithium because it substitutes for sodium in active transport. The triple combination of lithium + ACEI + NSAID is the classic Australian general-practice prescribing trap. Source: AMH; eTG Toxicology; Murray's Toxicology Handbook.

options.text: Lithium is hepatically metabolised by CYP3A4 and accumulates when CYP3A4 inhibitors are added; clarithromycin and grapefruit juice are the classic triggers
options.rationale: Lithium is NOT hepatically metabolised - it is purely renally excreted unchanged. CYP3A4 is irrelevant. Source: AMH.

options.text: Lithium chelates calcium and reduces parathyroid hormone production; chronic toxicity produces hypocalcaemic tetany and Trousseau sign
options.rationale: Lithium actually INCREASES PTH (causes hyperparathyroidism over years), producing hypercalcaemia not hypocalcaemia. Tetany is not the clinical picture described. Source: AMH.

options.text: Lithium directly inhibits muscarinic receptors causing central anticholinergic toxicity; trigger is co-administration with other antimuscarinic agents
options.rationale: Lithium is not an antimuscarinic. Anticholinergic toxidrome is different (hot, dry, flushed, mydriasis, ileus) and not the cerebellar-extrapyramidal picture described. Source: eTG Toxicology.

explanation.summary: Lithium is purely renally cleared. Its handling follows sodium: it is freely filtered and ~80 percent is reabsorbed in the proximal tubule by sodium-coupled mechanisms. Anything that reduces GFR (NSAID afferent constriction, ACEI efferent dilation in low-perfusion states) or upregulates proximal sodium reabsorption (volume depletion, low-salt diet, thiazide) causes lithium reabsorption to climb and serum levels to rise. The narrow therapeutic index (0.6-1.0 mmol/L; toxicity 1.5+; severe 2.5+) and the neurotoxic accumulation (substitutes for sodium in CNS Na-coupled transport, accumulates in Purkinje cells and basal ganglia) produce the classic cerebellar + extrapyramidal syndrome. Management: stop lithium, stop the precipitant (NSAID, ACEI), aggressive IV crystalloid to restore renal lithium clearance, haemodialysis when level >4.0 mmol/L acute or >2.5 with neurological signs (lithium has small Vd, dialyses readily).
key_points: Lithium = 100% renal clearance; never hepatic
Triple-trap interaction: lithium + NSAID + ACEI (or diuretic)
Therapeutic 0.6-1.0; toxicity >1.5; severe >2.5
Dialysis indications: level >4 acute, or >2.5 with neuro signs, or AKI
Long-term lithium adverse: nephrogenic DI, hypothyroidism, hyperparathyroidism, renal interstitial fibrosis
explanation.pearls: When commencing any new medication for a patient on lithium, ask: does this drug change renal sodium handling? If yes, monitor lithium level within 1-2 weeks. The classic exam vignette is the patient with new-onset hypertension started on an ACEI without lithium re-monitoring.

subtopic_detail: Lithium toxicity mechanism + drug interaction predictability
